summary: McKenzie lists the feature roadmap for version 1.04 of Bingo Card Creator, prompted by customer feedback. The update includes visual improvements, font choices, word wrap options, page positioning, and calling card printing. He teases readers with a puzzle about development time, noting one feature took 4 hours while another took just 5 minutes. The post illustrates the unpredictable nature of software development effort.
key_points: Customer feedback directly drove the feature priorities for the next release
The gap between perceived complexity and actual implementation time is often counterintuitive — one feature took 4 hours, another 5 minutes
Some features are flagged as doubtful for the release, showing disciplined scope management even while adding features
The post implicitly demonstrates the tension between responding to customer requests and managing feature creep
money_quotes: A customer convinced me to throw another one on the barbie, as it were.
One of the features on this list took 4 solid hours to implement. Another took 5 minutes. Any guesses which was which?
key_insight: The actual development cost of features is wildly unpredictable and often inversely correlated with how complex they appear to users.
